attributes.stem:
A 65-year-old man presents with a four hour history of severe pain in his right groin. This pain occurred while lying on the couch and is associated with an episode of vomiting shortly after. He has had a swelling of his right groin in the past six months. He has not opened his bowels over the last two days ago and feels constipated. His history includes chronic obstructive pulmonary disease for which he takes regular tiotropium. Eight years ago he had a laparotomy for small bowel obstruction.

On examination, he appears uncomfortable. His temperature is 37.8C and he is haemodynamically stable. His abdomen is distended and there is a tender, warm-to-touch, erythematous and tense lump measuring 4 x 4cm. The lump is located along the right inguinal ligament extending into his scrotum. Coughing and standing upright does not cause the lump to bulge further. Lying supine does not reduce the lump. The abdomen is distended and bowel sounds are evident.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a sudden onset of groin pain in the setting of a six month history of a lump located along his right groin. This is suggestive of a inguinal hernia. This patient almost certainly has a chronic cough associated with his chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and this is a risk factor for abdominal wall herniation. The associated symptoms and signs of bowel obstruction i.e. vomiting, bowels not opening for the past 48 hours, a distended abdomen as well as an inguinal bulge with overlying warmth and erythema would indicate that this inguinal hernia is strangulated with concurrent bowel obstruction. If no prompt action is taken, there is a real risk of ischaemic changes to the segment of intestine trapped in the hernia.

Prompt surgical repair is indicated in patients with complicated inguinal hernias such as incarcerated hernias and/or bowel obstruction. It is important that this patient undergoes emergency surgery to avoid possible bowel necrosis. An open (groin) approach used to be favoured as compared to a laparoscopic approach as it was associated with decreased risk of bowel injury and it provides a wider field of vision of surrounding structures. More recent studies would suggest that a laparoscopic approach is just as effective and often results in a more rapid post-operative recovery.

However in this case, with his history of a laparotomy for small bowel obstruction, the patient will almost certainly have widespread intra-abdominal adhesions, making a laparoscopic approach both challenging and risky. If there is evidence of ischaemic injury to any bowel trapped in the hernia, then a laparotomy may be needed to address this problem.

With regards to the hernia itself, a synthetic mesh is placed in between the abdominal internal oblique muscle and aponeurosis of the abdominal external oblique muscle. This open surgical technique is known as the Lichtenstein repair. On the other hand, a nonmesh repair by fixating the transverse fascia to the abdominal internal oblique muscle and transverse muscle at the inguinal ligament is another common open surgical technique. This is known as the Shouldice procedure.

Manual reduction under general anaesthetic or sedation is another method of treating incarcerated inguinal hernias without bowel obstruction. It may be attempted in patients without any clinical evidence of strangulation who have contraindications to emergency surgery. However, this patient presents with symptoms of strangulation and bowel obstruction and requires urgent surgical intervention.

The decision to proceed with surgery should be a clinical one. In this case scenario, the diagnosis is very obvious and any imaging investigation, such as an ultrasound or CT scan, is not going to influence management. Likewise, undertaking a diagnostic manoeuvre, such as a fine needle aspiration, would only complicate matters.
learningPoints: In situations where there has been previous abdominal surgery, complicated inguinal hernias are generally repaired using an open approach with a mesh repair
references: McCormack K, Scott NW, Go PM, et al. Laparoscopic techniques versus open techniques for inguinal hernia repair. Cochrane Database Syst Rev 2003; :CD001785.
Mahon D, Decadt B, Rhodes M. Prospective randomized trial of laparoscopic (transabdominal preperitoneal) vs open (mesh) repair for bilateral and recurrent inguinal hernia. Surg Endosc 2003; 17:1386.
O'Reilly EA, Burke JP, O'Connell PR. A meta-analysis of surgical morbidity and recurrence after laparoscopic and open repair of primary unilateral inguinal hernia. Ann Surg 2012; 255:846.

Jiang X, Sun R, Huang W, et al. Prospective comparison of two surgical approaches for incarcerated and strangulated inguinal hernia: preperitoneal hernioplasty through the lower abdominal median incision and laparoscope (TAPP). Updates in Surgery 2024; 76:2603–610
